summary: An excerpt from Ron Chernow's biography 'Titan' detailing John D. Rockefeller's family history, particularly his grandparents and his father. It highlights Rockefeller's pride in his humble American roots and his early development of a preference for sober, emotionally controlled associates over jovial but feckless characters.

principles.statement: Value good old American stock over manufactured noble lineage.
principles.rationale: He had no desire to trace his ancestry back to European nobility, preferring the honest reality of his American roots.
principles.source_quote: I have no desire to trace myself back to the nobility. I am satisfied with my good old American stock.

principles.statement: Prefer the society of sober, tight-lipped men in full command of their emotions.
principles.rationale: Observing his jovial but feckless grandfather taught him to equate bonhomie with a lax character.

signature_quotes.text: I have no desire to trace myself back to the nobility. I am satisfied with my good old American stock.
signature_quotes.context: Responding to genealogists offering to manufacture a royal lineage for him.

signature_quotes.text: My grandmother was a brave woman. Her husband was not so brave as she.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the mismatched character of his grandparents.

signature_quotes.text: In those days one who had his farm paid for and had a little money beside was counted rich.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Rockefeller equated a jovial, good-natured demeanor with a lax character and fecklessness, based on the behavior of his grandfather.
